coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive visual detection of H2O2 and glucose based on B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD ternary system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yaxin
Chen, Mei
Lu, Hongzhi
Xu, Shoufang
coredata.dc:description: A straightforward and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor comprising blue emissive carbon dots, silver nanoprisms, and o-phenylenediamine (B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD) was developed for the detection of H2O2 and glucose. Ag NPRs were etched into Ag+ ions upon exposure to H2O2, which caused non-fluorescent OPD to be oxidized and produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), resulting in intense yellow fluorescence emission at 555 nm. DAP can quench the B-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE), leading to a decrease in their fluorescence intensity at 435 nm. Because glucose oxidase may catalyze the oxidation of glucose to produce H2O2, this approach can also be used to detect glucose. The unique triangular structure leads to the easy etching of the edge and tip of Ag NPRs by H2O2 into Ag+ ions, allowing for the high sensitivity of detection of H2O2 and glucose with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 μM and 0.6 μM, respectively. Furthermore, with the increase of the concentrations of H2O2 and glucose, a visible color shift from blue to yellow-green occurs, which can be detected using a smartphone's color recognition application that captures the RGB channel values of the image. Real-time and on-spot detection of H2O2 and glucose were achieved based on smartphone sensing platform, presenting vast potential for applications in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Silver nanoprisms
H2O2
Glucose
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of B-CDs and Ag NPRs
Determination of H2O2 in real samples using fluorescence spectrum
Glucose determination in real samples using fluorescence spectrum
Determination of real samples by smartphone color recognition software
The smartphone color recognition platform detects H2O2
The smartphone color recognition platform for detection of glucose

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FL excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs, pictures of the B-CDs in daylight (left) and UV radiation (right) are included in the inset; (B) TEM image of Ag NPRs. Inset: Size distribution of Ag NPRs; (C) The TEM image of Ag NPRs after adding H2O2 for 30 min (3 μM). (D) The UV–Vis absorption of the Ag NPRs with different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 10, 50, 100, 400, 600, 800, 1000 and 12000 μM); Inset: Color change of Ag NPRs solution after addition with different concentrations of H2O2.

(A) The fluorescence spectra before and after the addition of H2O2, Ag+ and Ag NPRs were measured using 10 mM OPD solution as a substrate. (B) FL emission spectra of B-CDs along with UV–Vis absorption of DAP and Ag NPRs. (C) The quenching effect of different concentrations of DAP (0,2,4,6,8,10,15 and 20 μM) on carbon dots. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra with different reagents; Inset: Color change of a-j solution under.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and OPD that incubated with increasing concentration of H2O2, namely 0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 and 1200 μM, respectively; The calibration curves of the sensing system's fluorescence intensity ratio (F555/F435) versus H2O2 concentration are shown in (B) and (C); (D) The CIE 1931 color diagram after adding H2O2; (E) The images of solution under ultraviolet irradiation after adding different concentrations of H2O2; (F) The response values of the developed probe were evaluated against various potential interferences using 100 μM of H2O2 and 1000 μM of other interferents.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and OPD that incubated with increasing concentration of glucose, namely 0, 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 μM, respectively; The calibration curves of the sensing system's fluorescence intensity ratio (F555/F435) versus glucose concentration are shown in (B) and (C); (D) The CIE 1931 color diagram after adding glucose; (E) The images of solution under ultraviolet irradiation after adding different concentrations of H2O2 glucose; (F) The comparison of fluorescence intensities upon the addition of equimolar concentrations of fructose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose to a glucose system at 1000 μM.

(A)the linear relationship between the G/B value and the H2O2 concentration (0–1200 μM). (B)–(D): Actual detection of tap water, drinking water and lake water after adding different concentrations of H2O2 solution by color recognition software.

(A) The linear relationship between the G/B value and the glucose concentration (0–1000 μM); (B)–(D): Actual detection of human saliva after adding different concentrations of glucose solution by color recognition software.
Schematic illustration of the B-CDs-Ag NPR-OPD system for detection of H2O2 and glucose.
Determination of H2O2 in water samples using fluorescence spectrum.
Determination of glucose concentrations in salivary samples.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive visual detection of H2O2 and glucose based on B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD ternary system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Functional Polymers, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Functional Polymers, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward and highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor comprising blue emissive carbon dots, silver nanoprisms, and o-phenylenediamine (B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD) was developed for the detection of H2O2 and glucose. Ag NPRs were etched into Ag+ ions upon exposure to H2O2, which caused non-fluorescent OPD to be oxidized and produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), resulting in intense yellow fluorescence emission at 555 nm. DAP can quench the B-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE), leading to a decrease in their fluorescence intensity at 435 nm. Because glucose oxidase may catalyze the oxidation of glucose to produce H2O2, this approach can also be used to detect glucose. The unique triangular structure leads to the easy etching of the edge and tip of Ag NPRs by H2O2 into Ag+ ions, allowing for the high sensitivity of detection of H2O2 and glucose with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 μM and 0.6 μM, respectively. Furthermore, with the increase of the concentrations of H2O2 and glucose, a visible color shift from blue to yellow-green occurs, which can be detected using a smartphone's color recognition application that captures the RGB channel values of the image. Real-time and on-spot detection of H2O2 and glucose were achieved based on smartphone sensing platform, presenting vast potential for applications in various fields.

Blue carbon dots-silver nanoprisms-o-phenylenediamine (B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD) ternary system was proposed for ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose. Real-time and on-spot detection of H2O2 and glucose were achieved based on smartphone sensing platform.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important chemical raw material, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely utilized in medicine, industry, food, biology, and other fields. However, the excessive use of H2O2 can be detrimental to human health, causing cell cancer, accelerated aging, and cardiovascular disease [1,2]. Owing to these adverse effects, many countries have imposed stringent regulations on the permissible residue levels of H2O2 in food products. As per the guidelines of the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the residual H2O2 concentration in the final product must not exceed 0.5 mg L− 1. In Japan, protocols mandate the breakdown or removal of H2O2 before products leave the factory, while in China, food regulations strictly prohibit any residual H2O2 [3]. Given that glucose is the primary energy source for cells, fluctuations in glucose levels within the human body can contribute to the onset of various illnesses [4,5]. In the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose can be converted to H2O2, so that the content of glucose can be determined by measuring the amount of H2O2 produced [6]. The glucose level in saliva was positively correlated with the glucose level in blood [7]. Compared with blood collection, saliva collection can be completed at home or in a non-medical environment, which improves the convenience and accessibility of detection, and does not need to puncture the skin or extract blood, reducing the patient ‘s discomfort [8,9]. However, the glucose content in saliva is lower than that in blood [10]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop sensors with high sensitivity and low detection limit to detect H2O2 and glucose. Presently, photoelectrochemical technique [11], high performance liquid chromatography [12], electrochemical analysis [13], iodometric method [14] and colorimetric [15] have been used to detect H2O2 and glucose. The fluorescence approach has drawn a lot of attention because of its great sensitivity and selectivity [16]. When compared to single signal output fluorescence sensors, the ratiometric fluorescence probe (RFP) yielding high detection accuracy have shown a great potential due to its self-calibration feature [17]. Notably, the RFP provide a target-dependent fluorescence color change to make the on-site visual inspection easier.

Three categories can be distinguished in the RFP for detecting H2O2. (1) Based on the oxidation or elimination reaction of H2O2, many RFPs with small molecule fluorescent probes were designed [18–20]. For instance, H2O2 was detected using a bifunctional fluorescence sensor based on arylboronate-pyridinium [14] and a fluorescent probe DFB-1 that targets mitochondria and is based on curcuminoid difluoroboron [19]. However, the synthesis of these fluorescent probes is complex, and the reaction process may be susceptible to disruptions from external factors. (2) Based on the fact that H2O2 was used as an electron acceptor when peroxidase enzyme oxidizes the substrate, many RFPs were designed [21–23]. For example, Fe/N-CDs-OPD system [21], Cu-CDs-OPD system were designed for detection of H2O2 [22]. With the help of the peroxidase enzyme activity of the metal-doped CDs, H2O2 can be catalyzed to produce •OH, which can then oxidize OPD to produce DAP, which emits a strong yellow fluorescence, quenching CDs according to IFE. (3) A large number of RFPs were designed by taking advantage of the fact that H2O2 is capable of etching Ag NPs into silver ions [24–26]. For example, our group designed B-CDs-Ag NPs-QDs ternary system [26] for detection H2O2. In the absence of H2O2, the B-CDs were quenched by Ag NPs based on IFE, while QDs remained its red fluorescence. Ag NPs were etched into Ag+ ions in the presence of H2O2, causing the quenching of the QDs through electron transfer, while the blue CDs were restored.

Among the three commonly used strategies, utilization of noble metal nanoparticles and nano-enzyme was capable of amplifying the sensor sensitivity and decreasing the detection limit [27]. Combined the oxidation property of Ag+ [28] and Ag NPs can be etched by H2O2, Wang's group [29] designed Ag NPs-UIO-66-NH2-OPD system for detection of H2O2. In the presence of H2O2, Ag NPs undergo etching and are converted into Ag+ ions, which then lead to the in-situ conversion of OPD to DAP, and DAP in turn quench UIO-66-NH2 based on IFE. However, the quenching of UIO-66-NH2 was slight, the blue emissive UIO-66-NH2 was quenched by Ag NPs through IFE, but its fluorescence was restored upon the etching of Ag NPs.

Motivated by the works listed above, in this work, blue-carbon dots-silver nanoprisms-o-phenylenediamine (B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD) ternary system was designed for detection of H2O2 based on the H2O2 induced etching of Ag NPRs and the IFE between B-CDs and DAP. Scheme 1 illustrates how H2O2 oxidizes Ag NPRs to Ag+, which in turn converts OPD to DAP in situ and emits a yellow fluorescence at 555 nm, and DAP in turn quench B-CDs with emission at 435 nm based on IFE. While the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs gradually diminishes, the fluorescence intensity of DAP steadily increases as the concentration of H2O2 increases. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the solution's fluorescence color shifts from blue to yellow-green, enabling visual detection of H2O2 via smartphone. The following features of this study demonstrated its innovation: First, Ag NPRs were employed to replace the commonly used Ag NPs. Ag NPRs possess a triangular morphology with high surface energy compared to Ag NPs. Silver atoms in the tip and edge are more likely to coordinately engage with organic halogens [30] or oxidase produced by H2O2 [31], leading to change in size and morphology, as well as color shifts and UV–Vis absorption [32–35]. Second, the absorbance spectrum of Ag NPRs was in the range of 550–700 nm, avoiding quenching of the B-CDs. Therefore, the quench of B-CDs by DAP would be more obviously. Third, B-CDs were chosen for their easy preparation and high stability. Fourth, RFP was comprised by simple physical mixing, which avoid the complicated chemical bond or surface modification.

Ethylenediamine, sodium citrate, iron(Ⅲ) chloride hrxahydrate, glucose oxidase, polyvinylpyrrolidone, OPD and sodium borohydride were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. in Shanghai, China. Silver nitrate, sodium hydroxide, magnesium sulfate, copper(Ⅱ) sulfate pentahydrate, sulfuric acid, Nitric acid and glucose were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used to prepare all of the solutions.

Using a Horiba Scientific Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer, the fluorescence spectrum was captured. The UV-3600 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to obtain UV–vis absorption spectra. Morphological evaluation was performed using TEM, JEM-2100F. Images of fluorescence were obtained with a portable UV lamp, AGL-9406.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B-CDs and Ag NPRs
ce:section.ce:para: Using oxalate dihydrate and anhydrous ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, B-CDs were created through a one-step hydrothermal process [36]. Ag NPRs were synthesized by using PVP and NaBH4 as surface protecting agent and reducing agent, respectively [37]. The detailed steps were listed in the Supporting information.

The typical H2O2 detection process is as follows. First, 12 μM Ag NPRs solution and 300 μL of 10 mM OPD solution were combined with 50 μL of B-CDs solution. With the use of a phosphate buffered saline buffer (0.15 mM), the pH of the solution was brought to 8. After then, various H2O2 solution concentrations were added until the entire volume reached 3 mL. After 120 min of incubation at 25 °C, the resultant solution's fluorescence emission spectrum was captured.

To generate H2O2, 100 μL of 0.006 mg mL− 1 GOx solution was combined with different concentrations of glucose aqueous solution, then incubated for 80 min at 37 °C. According to the above steps, 50 μL of B-CDs solution, 12 μM Ag NPRs solution and 300 μL of 10 mM OPD solution were added to the solution. Then the pH was adjusted to 8 with phosphate buffered saline buffer (0.15 mM), and the mixture was allowed to react for 90 min at 25 °C.

The lake water was taken from the artificial lake of Linyi University. The H2O2 content of samples of tap water, drinking water, and actual lake water was measured using the standard addition method. The supernatant was extracted after centrifuging the collected water samples for 10 min at 8000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted ten-fold with ultrapure water, and the pH was adjusted to 8 using phosphate buffer. In the afore mentioned samples, 50 μL of B-CDs solution, 12 μM Ag NPRs solution, 300 μL of 10 mM OPD solution, and H2O2 solution were added with the finally concentration varied from 0 to 1200 μM for fluorescence detection.

Saliva samples were obtained from three healthy adults who refrained from consuming any food for 3 h prior to sampling [38]. The collected saliva was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm of 10 min, then the supernatant was collected and diluted ten-fold with ultrapure water. Subsequently, 0.006 mg mL− 1 glucose oxidase was introduced into the solution, after which different concentrations of glucose solution were added and reacted at 37 °C for 80 min. The final step entailed the inclusion of 30 μL B-CDs solution, 12 μM Ag NPRs solution and 300 μL of 10 mM OPD solution into the solution described above, while adjusting the pH to 8 with phosphate buffer. Thereafter, the reaction was administered at a temperature of 25 °C for 90 min before the fluorescence spectrum was recorded.

The synthesis of the B-CDs was carried out via a straightforward hydrothermal technique. As illustrated in Figure S1, the intensity of the emission peak for B-CDs gradually diminishes as the excitation wavelength increases. Conversely, the emission peak for DAP proportionally increased. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the emission peak heights of B-CDs and DAP are comparable, making it the optimal condition for subsequent H2O2 detection. The maximum emission peak of B-CDs is located at 435 nm when excited at 360 nm. Upon direct exposure to sunlight, an aqueous solution of B-CDs appears as a yellow-transparent liquid. However, under UV light at 365 nm, a fascinatingly bright-blue fluorescence can be observed (Fig. 1 A).

The TEM image of Ag NPRs was displayed in Fig. 1B. The shape of Ag NPRs was triangular, with a diameter of about 40 nm. Upon exposure to H2O2, the size of Ag NPRs is significantly reduced, and their shape changed from triangular to circular particles, as demonstrated in Fig. 1C, suggesting that Ag NPR tips are particularly susceptible to H2O2 etching. Ag NPRs' absorption peak at 650 nm was weakened and subsequently vanished upon the addition of H2O2, and the Ag NPRs solution's color changed from blue to colorless (Fig. 1D). The etching process may be construed as follows [35]:
2 Ag + H2O2 +2 H+ → 2 Ag+ +2 H2O

Initially, the assessment of the oxidation of OPD by Ag+ was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, when varying concentrations of Ag+ (0.3, 0.5 μM) were incubated with 10 mM OPD for 120 min, an incremental trend in the appearance and magnitude of a fresh peak at 555 nm was observed under 360 nm excitation as the Ag+ concentration rose. No such peaks were observed when Ag+ or OPD was present alone, or when Ag NPRs coexisted with OPD. However, in the presence of Ag NPRs, OPD, and H2O2, a distinct new peak emerged at 555 nm, validating the ability of Ag+ to oxidize OPD and elicit this novel peak.

Fig. 2B demonstrated that DAP can quench the fluorescence of B-CDs because of the strong coincidence between its UV–vis absorption (fluorescence excitation) spectra and the emission spectrum of the latter. As depicted in Fig, 2C, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 435 nm gradually decreased, whereas the intensity of the DAP at 555 nm increased with the rise in DAP concentration. Additionally, the absorption spectrum of Ag NPRs was observed at 650 nm, which does not overlap with the emission spectrum of B-CDs, making the impact of Ag NPRs on the fluorescence of B-CDs negligible.

According to Figure S2, it is observed that with the increase in H2O2 concentration, there is no change in the emission peaks of B-CDs, DAP and OPD, indicating that the addition of H2O2 does not disrupt the stability of the entire system. Further information is presented in Fig. 2D. When B-CDs are individually exposed to Ag NPRs, OPD, H2O2, and Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remained constant. However, when B-CDs, Ag NPs, OPD and H2O2 coexist, a distinct emission peak at 555 nm can be observed, accompanied by a decrease in the emission peak of B-CDs at 435 nm. Moreover, the same effect can be achieved when B-CDs coexist with OPD and Ag+. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the solution's hue changed from blue to yellow-green following the sensor system's reaction with H2O2. These observations indicate that the addition of H2O2 causes Ag NPRs to break down into Ag+, leading to the oxidation of OPD and the production of DAP, which visibly displays colour and fluorescence signals. The construction of an IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform is made possible by the partial overlap of the UV absorption peak of DAP with the emission peak of B-CDs.

A ratio of the fluorescence intensity of DAP and B-CDs (F555/F435) was utilized to evaluate the detection outcomes. Several experimental parameters, including OPD concentration, pH value, Ag NPRs concentration, and incubation time, were tuned to achieve optimal H2O2 sensing performance. Firstly, the influence of OPD concentration on the system was examined at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the sensor consistently rose with increasing OPD concentration. The fluorescence intensity ratio peaked at 10 mM OPD concentration before reaching a plateau, as shown in Figure S3A. Some sensors are only capable of detecting specific pH values, which may affect their subsequent applications. Accordingly, the pH value detection of the sensor should be analyzed in detail during the detection process. The amino group of OPD can be protonated in a strong acidic solution, which will prevent Ag from oxidizing [39]. Therefore, the pH was meticulously optimized using phosphate-buffered saline buffer within the range of 6.0–8.5. The fluorescence intensity ratio increases steadily with increasing pH levels. At pH 8.0, the fluorescence intensity ratio peaked before gradually declining. The reason may be due to the hydrolysis of Ag, which inhibits the oxidation ability of Ag+. Hence, pH 8.0 was determined as the optimal value (Figure S3B). Subsequently, the influence of the concentration of Ag NPRs on the fluorescence intensity ratio was analyzed. As the concentration of Ag NPRs increased, the fluorescence intensity ratio also increased gradually. After the concentration of Ag NPRs reached 12 μM, the rate of increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio slowed down, indicating that 12 μM of Ag NPRs was adequate to consume 100 μM of H2O2 (Fig. S3C). Following 120 min of optimal incubation, it was discovered that the fluorescence intensity ratio tended to stabilize (Fig. S3C).

On the basis of optimized conditions, B-CDs-AgNPR-OPD fluorescence was utilized for H2O2 detection. As shown in Fig. 3 A, a strong linear relationship between F555/F435 and H2O2 concentration was observed. The sensor's fluorescence intensity declines at 435 nm and increases at 555 nm when the H2O2 concentration increases from 0 to 1200 μM. When the concentration of H2O2 exceeds 1200 μM, the change of fluorescence intensity ratio gradually slows down (Fig. 3B). Y = 0.00675 X + 0.172 (R2 = 0.99743) is the linear regression equation based on a signal to noise ratio (S/N) of 3. The detection limit was 0.12 μM (Fig. 3C), which is much lower than the maximum residual concentration (0.5 mg L− 1) set by the FDA. Notably, during this process, the solution's color alters from blue to yellow upon exposure to 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 3E), such alterations are confirmed via the CIE diagram (Fig. 3D). The significant color changes provide a basis for the visual detection of H2O2.

The sensor's practical application relies heavily on its selectivity. As a result, we conducted a study on potential interfering substances in water, including Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-, SO4 2 -, glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH) and glycine (Gly). As shown in Fig. 3F, the oxidation of OPD to DAP can occur due to the oxidizing properties of Cu2+, Mg2+ and other metal ions, leading to a slight increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio beyond the blank value [39–41]. Among the tested analytes, the F555/F435 value exhibited the most significant rise upon the addition of H2O2, indicating the exceptional selectivity of the B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD ternary-component system as a probe for detecting H2O2.

Due to the ability of GOx to generate H2O2 by oxidizing glucose, the ratiometric probe is expected to exhibit excellent performance in detecting glucose. In order to attain optimal analytical outcomes, a detailed analysis was conducted on the effects of four critical parameters on the ratiometric metrics: GOx concentration, pH, incubation time of glucose oxidase and response time. As seen in Figure S4, the fluorescence intensity ratio reaches its maximum at a pH of 8.0, the glucose oxidase concentration of 0.006 mg mL− 1, and the glucose oxidase incubation time was 80 min. After an 90-min reaction period, the fluorescence intensity ratio remains relatively stable. Therefore, 0.006 mg mL− 1 GOx was used in subsequent experiments, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 with phosphate buffer. In addition, the intensity ratio tends to be stable after 90 min, which is considered to be the preferred incubation time for glucose detection.

As depicted in Fig. 4 A, under ideal conditions, the sensor's fluorescence intensity decreased at 435 nm and increased at 555 nm as the glucose concentration increased from 0 to 1000 μM. Beyond 1000 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio shows a slower rate of change (Fig. 4B). Additionally, a robust linear relationship was observed between the ratio of F 555 to F 435 and the glucose concentration. The response values and glucose concentrations have a linear relationship, as seen in Fig. 4C. The obtained curves can be fitted using the following regression equation: Y = 0.00156 X +0.16829 (R2 = 0.99698), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 μM, providing an estimated method for glucose determination within the range of 0–1000 μM. The solution's fluorescence color shifted from blue to yellow-green as the content of glucose increased (Fig. 4D). Additionally, Fig. 4E illustrates the color transformation of the solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

In terms of the selectivity of proportional probes, when coexisting with other sugars (fructose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose), the selectivity of F 555/F 435 was not significantly different from that when glucose was only added to 1000 μM glucose solution (Fig. 4F). This notable resistance to interference can be attributed to the specific interaction between glucose and GOx, thereby enhancing the precision and dependability of the outcomes. Furthermore, compared with the developed H2O2 and glucose monitoring methods, the sensor has lower LOD and wider linear range (Table S1). In addition, when compared to probes developed for H2O2 and glucose detection, Ag NPRs are more easily etched by H2O2 due to their sharp morphology. Compared with spherical Ag NPs, Ag NPRs are more sensitive to H2O2. As shown in Table S1, B-CDs-Ag NPRs-OPD ternary-component sensors have lower LOD and wider linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 in real samples using fluorescence spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 level was measured using a variety of real samples, including lake water, drinking water, and tap water, in order to evaluate the viability of the proposed probe. Specifically, different concentrations of H2O2 solution were added to the sample to obtain 10 μM, 40 μM and 60 μM H2O2 mixture. Subsequently, the prepared samples underwent analysis using the standard addition method, and the findings were recorded in Table 1 . The RSD were less than 3.4 %, and the recovery rates varied from 97.6 % to 109.8 %. These outcomes affirm that the proposed method can be effectively leveraged in the precise determination of H2O2 in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose determination in real samples using fluorescence spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of this approach for detecting glucose in real samples was validated by detecting glucose levels in human saliva. The saliva of three healthy adults was diluted 10 times, and different concentrations of glucose solution were added to the sample using standard addition method. Glucose mixtures of 10 μM, 40 μM and 60 μM were obtained. Subsequent analysis of these samples was carried out according to the established protocol. The results are presented in Table 2 , showing RSD values were less than 1.0 % and the recoveries varied from 96.4 % to 118.2 %. These outcomes explicitly indicate that the developed approach can successfully be utilized for determining glucose levels in practical samples with high accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of real samples by smartphone color recognition software
The smartphone color recognition platform detects H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Previous research has witnessed limited utilization of strip-based analytical methods primarily for semi-quantitative monitoring, relying on the human eye's capability to perceive subtle color variations However, the demands of accurate quantitative monitoring are frequently beyond the capabilities of current quick detection methods. The utilization of color image extraction on smartphones and RGB numerical analysis applications has facilitated the quantitative analysis of fluorescent colors. As shown in Fig. 5 A, this application may measure the relative chromatism of blue and green in the test paper sensor's fluorescence color before and after adding glucose and H2O2, so as to realize accurate and intelligent detection of H2O2 and glucose. The smartphone's color recognition software detected the color of the solution after different amounts of hydrogen peroxide were added to the mixture. It is clear that the blue color of the solution eventually turned to a yellow-green color. Moreover, there was a strong linear correlation between the G/B ratio and the H2O2 concentration; the linear regression equation was Y = 0.00512 X + 0.539 (R2 = 0.99334).

This software makes it easier to detect H2O2 with accuracy and intelligence by analyzing the relative chromas of blue and green in the fluorescence color of the water sample solution before and after adding H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5B–D, the standard addition method was used to evaluate the sensor. After adding H2O2 to the actual sample at a concentration of 0,10,40,60 μM, there was a strong linear relationship between the G/B of the fluorescence color of the solution and the concentration of H2O2. The recovery rate of H2O2 was found to be between 91.23 % and 121.1 %, with an RSD of less than 4.2 %, hence evidencing the method's capacity and precision in detection. (Table. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The smartphone color recognition platform for detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The colour recognition software of the smartphone can discern the fluorescence colour of the solution after adding different quantities of glucose. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the naked eye can easily perceive the transition in color from blue to yellow-green. Notably, the G/B value of the color exhibits a favorable linear relationship with the glucose concentration, as evidenced by the linear regression equation Y = 0.0042 X + 0.56315 (R2 = 0.99922).

As shown in Fig. 6B–D, the software for color recognition in smartphones was employed to discern glucose levels in human saliva. Variable concentrations of glucose (0, 10, 40, 60 μM) were introduced into saliva by standard addition method. G/B presented a fine linear correlation with glucose concentration. The recovery rate ranged between 97.2 % and 120.4 %, and the relative standard deviation stood below 1.9 %, demonstrating strong dependability and accuracy (Table. S3). The developed sensing system is appropriate for real-world applications with real samples, as indicated by the lack of significant discrepancies between Table S2 and Table 1 and Table S3 and Table 2.

In conclusion, a dual emission ratio fluorescence sensor employing B-CDs, Ag NPRs, and OPD has been developed for the optical detection of H2O2 and glucose. As a reference signal, the blue fluorescence of B-CDs can be quelled by DAP through an internal filtration effect, resulting in a range of fluorescence color transitions from blue to yellow-green. This demonstrates a high sensitivity, with detection limits established at 0.12 μM and 0.6 μM for H2O2 and glucose, respectively. Moreover, the intelligent detection of H2O2 can be realized by using the RGB value in the smartphone colour recognition software, enabling the safe monitoring of H2O2 levels in lake, tap, and drinking water samples. Notably, glucose oxidase can convert glucose into hydrogen peroxide, allowing for the inference of high and low glucose levels in the human body by analyzing the glucose content in saliva. Additionally, the versatility of the ratio fluorescence sensor indicates its potential for expanded applications in detecting a range of H2O2-related analytes.
Yaxin Li: Writing – original draft. Mei Chen: Investigation. Hongzhi Lu: Data curation. Shoufang Xu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2024.120755.
